quiz.title: Grade-10 Physics: Unit 5 Quiz 8
quiz.subtitle: Forces in Circular Motion — Friction, Normal Force, and Gravity
quiz.subject: Physics

questions.question: A $1,200\text{ kg}$ car drives through a dip of radius $40\text{ m}$ at $12\text{ m/s}$. What is the normal force at the bottom? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
A ball is whirled in a horizontal circle on a frictionless table, attached to a string. What provides the centripetal force?
questions.explanation: The tension in the string pulls the ball toward the center of the circle, acting as the centripetal force.

questions.question: In a rotor ride, riders are pressed against the spinning wall. What force provides the centripetal force?
questions.explanation: The wall pushes inward on the riders — this normal force acts as the centripetal force. Friction holds them up, but does not provide $F_c$.

questions.question: The Moon orbits Earth in a nearly circular path. What force provides the centripetal force?
questions.explanation: Earth's gravitational pull on the Moon acts toward the center of the orbit, providing the centripetal force.

questions.question: A roller coaster car moves through a vertical loop. At the top of the loop, what force(s) provide the centripetal force?
options: Both gravity and the frictional force
questions.explanation: At the top, $mg + F_N = mv^2/r$. Both forces point toward the center. At the minimum speed to clear the loop, $F_N = 0$ and gravity alone provides $F_c$.

questions.question: A $900\text{ kg}$ car crests a hill of radius $50\text{ m}$ at $10\text{ m/s}$. What is the normal force at the top? ($g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$)
In uniform circular motion, the net force on the object is directed:
questions.explanation: The net force in uniform circular motion is the centripetal force, which always points toward the center of the circle.

questions.question: A car turns left on a flat road. The friction force on the tires acts:
options: Toward the center of the turn (left)
Away from the center of the turn (right)
questions.explanation: Friction points toward the center of the circular path, providing the centripetal force needed to turn left.

questions.question: A car drives through a dip in the road (concave upward). At the bottom of the dip, the normal force is:
questions.explanation: $F_N - mg = mv^2/r$, so $F_N > mg$. The road must push up harder than the car's weight to provide the upward centripetal force.

questions.question: A car drives over a hill (convex upward). At the top of the hill, the normal force is:
questions.explanation: $mg - F_N = mv^2/r$, so $F_N < mg$. The car feels lighter as part of its weight provides the centripetal force.

questions.question: A coin rests on a rotating turntable and moves in a circle without slipping. What force provides the centripetal force?
questions.explanation: Static friction between the coin and the turntable points toward the center, keeping the coin in circular motion.

questions.question: A stone on a string swings in a vertical circle. At which point is the tension greatest?
questions.explanation: At the bottom, $T = mg + mv^2/r$ — tension must support the weight AND provide centripetal force. At the top, $T = mv^2/r - mg$, so tension is least.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered identifying forces in circular motion scenarios!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the normal force in dips vs. hills and at the top vs. bottom of loops.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on identifying which force points toward the center in each scenario.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: centripetal force is a net force, not a new type — it's provided by gravity, tension, friction, or normal force.
